name: Clurichaun
description: The Clurichaun is a solitary fairy spirit from Irish folklore, often described as a mischievous and perpetually drunk cousin of the Leprechaun. Unlike the industrious Leprechaun who guards his pot of gold, the Clurichaun is known for his love of drink, revelry, and causing trouble. He is said to haunt wine cellars, breweries, and pubs, indulging in the finest spirits and wines. While not inherently malicious, a Clurichaun can become quite agitated if his demands for alcohol are not met, leading to poltergeist-like activity such as upsetting furniture, making loud noises, and even souring milk. Despite their disruptive behavior, some believe that a Clurichaun can also be a beneficial spirit to have around, as he is said to protect the wine cellar from theft and fire, provided he is treated with respect and given his due share of drink. He is often depicted as a small, wizened old man, dressed in tattered green or red clothing, wearing a leather apron, and sporting a long beard. Some tales suggest that if one manages to capture a Clurichaun, he can be forced to reveal the location of hidden treasure, though this is a difficult and risky endeavor. The Clurichaun embodies the more chaotic and whimsical side of Irish fairy lore, a reminder that even the smallest of creatures can wield considerable influence and mischief.
subtitle: The Drunken Cousin of the Leprechaun
appearance: A small, wizened old man, typically dressed in tattered green or red clothing, often including a leather apron. He sports a long, unkempt beard and a mischievous glint in his eye. He may also be seen wearing a cocked hat or riding on the backs of sheep or dogs.
habitat: Wine cellars, breweries, pubs, and any location where alcohol is stored or consumed. They can also be found in deserted castles or homes, often near their preferred drink.
behavior: Mischievous, drunken, and solitary. They are prone to causing trouble and playing pranks, especially if their demands for alcohol are not met. They are also protective of their chosen territory and will defend it against intruders. While generally solitary, they can form attachments to certain locations or individuals.

keyMyths.title: The Sour Milk
keyMyths.description: A farmer neglected to leave out a drink for the Clurichaun inhabiting his cellar, resulting in all the milk in the dairy turning sour overnight. He learned to appease the creature from then on. The source is from oral tradition and various collections of Irish folktales.

sources.author: W.B. Yeats
sources.work: Fairy and Folk Tales of the Irish Peasantry
sources.description: A collection of Irish folklore, including stories about Clurichauns.
sources.text: A collection of Irish folklore, including stories about Clurichauns.

sources.author: Patricia Lysaght
sources.description: Discusses various Irish fairies and their roles in folklore, including the Clurichaun.
